text: Dr. Safa explores the abdominal cavity, noting adhesions between the stomach, pancreas, and retroperitoneum due to the patient's previous sleeve gastrectomy. Using a 5mm ligature, he meticulously dissects these adhesions, liberating the upper part of the stomach, including the fundus. He then begins dissecting the entron, which is adherent to the transverse mesocolon.

Dr. Safa dissects the posterior part of the duodenum, reaching the pancreaticoduodenal pedicle. Using a Maryland Grasper, he performs the retroduodenal passage, ensuring no bleeding and preserving the pancreas.

Dr. Safa performs a sleeve gastrectomy using green endo-GIA staplers, calibrating the sleeve with a 40 French bougie. The enlarged sleeve from a previous procedure is adjusted. A 60mm blue cartridge on the GIA is used to section the duodenum 2 cm distal to the pylorus, with careful attention to avoid injuring the pancreas.

The surgeon locates the ileocecal junction and measures 2.5 meters along the ileum using graspers to determine the site for the duodenal ileal anastomosis. Barbed sutures are used to fix the ileum onto the duodenum.
